coredata.dc:title: Synthesizing carbon dots with functional preservation strategy as a facile ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform for monitoring hypochlorite in living cells and zebrafish
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Yunhao
Wu, Tian
Duan, Lizheng
Hu, Guizhen
Shi, Jiahui
Nie, Yamin
Zhou, Yanmei
coredata.dc:description: As an important species of reactive oxygen and a commonly-used water disinfectant, hypochlorite (ClO-) has been reported to be tightly associated with oxidative stress and related diseases. Therefore, developing ClO- sensors, especially the sensors that can detect in a ratiometric fluorescent manner are highly desirable. Herein, a ClO- ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform M-CDs which can respond to ClO- rapidly and precisely was subtly constructed with a detection range of 0.1–50 μM and a low detection limit of 67 nM by simply blending the green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) and red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs) with no complicated synthesis and surface modification. Both CDs were legitimately prepared by a functional preservation strategy, and they exhibited different ClO- reactivity due to the inheritance of functional groups and properties of different precursors. The detection mechanism was verified by Mass spectrometry, temperature-dependent fluorescence, fluorescence decay curves and 1H NMR spectra. Besides the excellent performance in water samples, the platform also enables the monitoring of ClO- in living cells and zebrafish, which indicates the sensing system pens up new horizons for the development of ratiometric fluorescent sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Preservation strategy
Neutral red
Carbon dots
Ratiometric strategy
ClO- detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: MTT assay for the cell cytotoxicity
Optimal working condition for ratiometric fluorescent platform
Response of individual CDs to ClO-
Proposed detection mechanism of ClO- based on M-CDs
Detection of ClO- in water samples
ClO- imaging in living cells and zebrafish

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorbance, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of (A) G-CDs and (B) R-CDs.
(A) The fluorescence spectrum of the ratiometric fluorescence probe in the presence of ClO-. (B) The linear relationship between F630/F525 and the concentrations of ClO-.

(A) The specificity of M-CDs to ClO- (50 μM) in absence and presence of interference ions (500 μM) were investigated. (1) Blank, (2) ClO-, (3) H2O2, (4) ONOO-, (5) •OH, (6) 1O2, (7) •O2 -, (8) Cys, (9) Hcy, (10) GSH, (11) Na+, (12) K+, (13) Fe3+, (14) Al3+, (15) Mg2+, (16) Zn2+, (17) Ba2+, (18) Hg2+, (19) Cl-, (20) F-, (21) SO4 2-. (B) The fluorescent spectra of M-CDs after and before the additions of analytes. (a) Without any addition of analytes. (b) The addition of 500 μM interference ions (3−21) together. (c) A further addition of 50 μM ClO- in (b).

Confocal images of A549 cells after the pre-treatment with (a) 1 mM NAC, (b) 0 µM, (c) 10 µM, (d) 30 µM and (e) 50 µM ClO- for 30 min followed by incubation with M-CDs for 1 h. (f) Relative average fluorescence intensity in the green channel and red channel measured by Image J. Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 5). Scale bar = 20 µm.

Confocal images of the A549 cells after the incubated with (a) PBS buffer for 8 h, (b) 200 μM ABAH, LPS (2 μg/mL) for 8 h and PMA (2 μg/mL) for 30 min, (c) LPS (1 μg/mL) for 8 h and PMA (1 μg/mL) for 30 min, (d) LPS (2 μg/mL) for 8 h and PMA (2 μg/mL) for 30 min followed by incubation with M-CDs for 1 h. (e) Relative average fluorescence intensity in the green channel and red channel measured by Image J. Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 5). Scale bar = 20 µm.

Fluorescent Inverted microscope imaging of ClO- in zebrafish. (a-d) Images of the M-CDs in zebrafish with the different concentrations of ClO- (0, 10, 30, and 50 μM). (e) Relative average fluorescence intensity in the green channel and red channel measured by Image J. Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 5). Scale bar = 500 µm.
Schematic illustration of ratiometric fluorescence platform design and its application as a nanoprobe for ratiometric ClO- detection.
Determination of ClO- in lake water and tap water.

head.ce:title: Synthesizing carbon dots with functional preservation strategy as a facile ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform for monitoring hypochlorite in living cells and zebrafish

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of environmental pollution control materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of environmental pollution control materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of environmental pollution control materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an important species of reactive oxygen and a commonly-used water disinfectant, hypochlorite (ClO-) has been reported to be tightly associated with oxidative stress and related diseases. Therefore, developing ClO- sensors, especially the sensors that can detect in a ratiometric fluorescent manner are highly desirable. Herein, a ClO- ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform M-CDs which can respond to ClO- rapidly and precisely was subtly constructed with a detection range of 0.1–50 μM and a low detection limit of 67 nM by simply blending the green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) and red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs) with no complicated synthesis and surface modification. Both CDs were legitimately prepared by a functional preservation strategy, and they exhibited different ClO- reactivity due to the inheritance of functional groups and properties of different precursors. The detection mechanism was verified by Mass spectrometry, temperature-dependent fluorescence, fluorescence decay curves and 1H NMR spectra. Besides the excellent performance in water samples, the platform also enables the monitoring of ClO- in living cells and zebrafish, which indicates the sensing system pens up new horizons for the development of ratiometric fluorescent sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: As an important reactive oxygen species (ROS), hypochlorite is not only used as a drinking water disinfectant in daily life, but also plays a key role in physiological and pathological processes [1], involving in redox homeostasis and biological information transmission [2] and other physiological processes. However, it is well-documented that abnormal levels of ClO- are associated with many diseases including arthritis, cardiovascular disease, and certain cancers [3]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a sensitive and rapid monitoring and analysis strategy.

Fluorescence imaging has appeared as an attractive technique due to its simplicity, high sensitivity and real-time non-invasive imaging capabilities. In particular, the development of ratiometric fluorescent probes has attracted a great deal of interest. Different from single-wavelength probes, ratiometric fluorescence probes can effectively eliminate background interference through the self-calibration of its own double emission band and thus has a higher signal-to-noise ratio and high precision. Up to now, a quantity of ratiometric fluorescent probes based on organic small molecule or carbon dots have been reported for monitoring ClO- [4–8]. Although these probes composed of small organic molecules have reached satisfactory detection limits, they often suffer from drawbacks of complex synthetic steps, low yields, poor photostability, and insurmountable toxicity. With superior water dispersibility, excellent photostability, and low toxicity [9,10], fluorescent carbon dots have attracted substantial attention in environmental monitoring and further bioimaging, which were considered to be ideal candidates of fluorescent sensors for ClO- detection. However, for carbon dots-based ratiometric fluorescent probes, elaborate design and complicated surface modification are generally required during the synthesis process. Accordingly, the preparation of a straightforward and efficient ClO- ratiometric probe is still facing great challenges.

There is sufficient evidence to show that under suitable pyrolysis temperature, precursor fragments on the surface of carbon dots can be retained without decomposition reaction, which is expected to maintain the properties of precursor molecules [11–13]. Meanwhile, there has been some excellent work [14,15] reporting that aniline compounds can be oxidized into nitroso compounds by ClO-. In light of the above characteristics, we synthesized green fluorescence ClO--inert G-CDs and red fluorescence intrinsic ClO--sensitive R-CDs with arylamine group through the functional preservation strategy [13]. The as-prepared R-CDs consists of the neutral red structure as luminescent centers and reaction sites for ClO- (Fig S1), while G-CDs acts as an internal standard [16]. A fluorescent ratiometric ClO- platform named M-CDs was designed and synthesized for the ClO- detection by simply blending G-CDs and R-CDs into one system to avoid complicated surface engineering and enhance detection accuracy by the self-calibration of the dual emission band. In the presence of ClO-, neutral red residues on the surface of R-CDs can be oxidized into nitroso compounds accompanied by red fluorescence quenching, while G-CDs cannot be selectively quenched, thus achieving the ratiometric fluorescence detection of ClO- ( Scheme 1).

Citric acid (CA), Triethylenetetramine (TETA) and Neutral red (NR) were purchased from Aladdin. MgSO4, CuCl2, HgCl2 and FeCl3 were obtained from Energy Chemical. L-2-Aminobutanamide hydrochloride (ABAH), N-Acetyl-cysteine (NAC) and Methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) were obtained from Aladdin. The UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired from U-4100 spectrophotometer and fluorescence emission spectra were obtained from Edinburgh FS5 spectrofluorometer. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were acquired on JEM-ARM300F. Bruker VERTEX 70 is applied to record FT-IR spectra. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were acquired from Thermo ESCALAB 250XI. The laser scanning confocal microscope (Olympus FV3000) was employed to obtain the cell images. Nikon Ti2-U inverted fluorescence microscope for zebrafish imaging.

CA and TETA are the carbon source for G-CDs. In brief, 500 mg of CA was added in 10 mL ultrapure water, and 500 μL of TETA was subsequently added. The solution is mixed evenly by ultrasound and then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) and reacted at 180 ℃ for 4 h. Then filter with a 220 nm aperture filter. The solution was transferred into the dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 18 h to remove the remaining small molecules. G-CDs powder were obtained through freeze-drying in vacuum.

R-CDs was prepared using our reported method with minor modifications [17]. Briefly, 3 g CA and 4.2 mg NR were added in 10 mL ultrapure water, and sonicated for 10 min, then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, a purple R-CDs solution was obtained, which was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter. Finally, solid R-CDs were obtained by lyophilization. For CA-CDs, all reaction conditions are the same as for R-CDs, except for the absence of NR.

The M-CDs were prepared by mixing G-CDs and R-CDs in PBS buffer (pH=7.4), and the concentration of G-CDs and R-CDs was adjusted with PBS buffer to 0.5 mg/mL and 2 mg/mL, respectively. Then varying concentrations of ClO- were separately added into the M-CDs solution and the final working volume is constant to 2.5 mL. The fluorescence spectra and temperature-dependent fluorescence were recorded by Edinburgh FS5 spectrofluorometer at 455 nm excitation. For actual sample analysis, lake water and tap water were obtained from the West Lake of Henan University and the laboratory respectively after filtrating by a 220 nm filter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MTT assay for the cell cytotoxicity
ce:section.ce:para: A549 cells were cultured in DMEM medium that were supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin-streptomycin at 37 °C with 5% CO2. 500 μL cells were placed in 96-plate for 24 h. The cells were incubation with 500 μL different concentrations of M-CDs (The concentrations of G-CDs were 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.5, 1, 2 mg/mL, while the concentrations of R-CDs were 0, 0.5, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3 mg/mL) for 6 h. The cells were then treated with 10 μL 5 mg/mL MTT for 4 h and 100 μL DMSO for 10 min. The absorbance at 570 nm were recorded.

A549 cells were cultured in DMEM. The cells were inoculated in confocal dish and cultured with reactive oxygen scavengers NAC (1 mM) and different concentrations of ClO- (0, 10, 30, 50 μM) at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 30 min. Subsequently, the cells were cultured with M-CDs (G-CDs and R-CDs were at the concentration of 0.5 mg/mL and 2 mg/mL respectively) at 37 °C for 1 h, and the confocal images were obtained from a confocal laser scanning microscope. For imaging endogenous hypochlorite, cells were treated with the myeloperoxidase inhibitor (ABAH) and hypochlorite stimulants LPS and PMA [4] at different concentrations. The 4-day-old wild zebrafish were treated with the steps described above at 28 ℃, and the images were obtained from a fluorescent Inverted microscope.

The morphology and size distribution of R-CDs and G-CDs were characterization by transmission electron microscopy. As shown in Fig S2A, the average particle size of G-CDs is 12.7 ± 3 nm. The HRTEM image of G-CDs (inserted in Fig S2A) shows a clear lattice structure with a lattice spacing of 0.25 nm, which corresponds to the (111) plane of graphitic carbon [18]. G-CDs displayed a wide diffraction peak (2θ) concentrated at 24°, indicating that the carbon nucleus is amorphous [19]. (Fig S3A) The G-CDs present two characteristic absorptions at 240 and 355 nm, which are ascribed to the π − π * transitions of the CC and C-N bond, respectively ( Fig. 1A) [20]. The fluorescence spectrum of the G-CDs has an emission peak at 525 nm under excitation of 455 nm. The FT-IR spectrum of G-CDs is shown in Fig S4A. The absorption band of the characteristic peak at 3450 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration [21] of N-H, and the characteristic peak at 2998 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration [22] of CH3. The peaks observed at 1630 cm−1 and 1490 cm−1 correspond to CO and C-N respectively [23], indicating that the surface of G-CDs contains amino groups, which endow them with good water solubility and the potential luminescence centers [24]. In addition, XPS spectra of G-CDs were also recorded (Fig S5A), indicating that G-CDs consist of C, N and O elements. C 1 s (Fig S5B) can be deconvolution into three peaks, and the peaks at 283.4 eV, 285 eV and 286.5 eV of binding energy can correspond to CC, C-C and C-O respectively [25]. The deconvolutions of the N 1 s (Fig S5C) band into two peaks, corresponding to C-N of 398.6 eV and N-H of 399.9 eV respectively. In the O 1 s spectrum, the peaks at 529.4 eV, 530.5 eV and 531.8 eV are related to C-O, CO and C-OH (Fig S5D) [26,27]. These results indicate that G-CDs contains -OH, -COOH and -NH2, which in accord with FT-IR spectra.

Transmission electron microscopy revealed that the R-CDs were 2–5 nm in diameter and had an ordered atomic structure. The average particle size of R-CDs is 3.4 ± 1.5 nm. The HRTEM image of R-CDs (inserted in Fig S2B) shows a clear lattice structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [28]. Compared with NR, the XRD of R-CDs displayed great and sharp diffraction peaks, indicating the crystalline nature of the R-CDs (Fig S3B). The R-CDs present two characteristic absorptions at 250 and 530 nm (Fig. 1B), which is considered as the π − π * transitions of the CC and aromatic π system, respectively [29]. It indicates that the R-CDs have molecular transition [30], which induces the red emission of R-CDs at 630 nm. The excitation and emission spectra of R-CDs, which are similar to NR and indicates that NR residues were successfully attached in the surface of the R-CDs [11]. The FT-IR spectrum of the R-CDs is given in Fig S4B. The band at around 3495, 1721 and 1211 cm−1 indicates the existence of O-H, CO and C-O-C, the peaks observed at 3296 cm−1 correspond to N-H. The peaks observed at 1551 cm−1 and 1412 cm−1 correspond to CC and C-N respectively [31,32]. We also synthesized CA-CDs with citric acid as a single precursor under the same reaction conditions. Based on the difference of FT-IR spectrum, the incorporation of NR onto the R-CDs was further confirmed, attributed to the N-H bond in NR [33]. In the XPS spectra (Fig S6), C1s (Fig S6B) can be deconvolution into three peaks at 284.9, 285.2, 286.4, 287.4 and 289.2 eV, are associated with CC, C-C, C-N, C-O, CO, respectively [20]. The peaks at 532.3, 533.1 and 533.8 eV, representing C-O, CO and C-OH/C-O-C, were observed from Fig S6C. The deconvolution of the N 1 s band into two peaks, corresponding to C-N of 399.7 eV and N-H of 401.6 eV respectively [34,35] (Fig S6D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal working condition for ratiometric fluorescent platform
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission of G-CDs reaches its maximum emission intensity at excitation wavelength of 455 nm, while R-CDs reaches its maximum emission intensity at 530 nm excitation and reaches half of the maximum emission intensity at 455 nm excitation (Fig S7). In the cause of ensure the simultaneous excitation of G-CDs and R-CDs, 455 nm was selected as the best excitation wavelength of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor. As can be seen from Fig S8, M-CDs have two emission peaks near 525 nm and 630 nm under excitation of 455 nm. The fluorescence intensity of M-CDs remained stable in the range of pH 2–8 and decayed in more acidic or more basic solutions, which can be understood in terms of protonation-deprotonation of -COOH and -NH2. Therefore, pH= 7.4 was selected as the working pH (Fig S9A). In addition, M-CDs can maintain good photostability even under continuous UV light irradiation (Fig S9B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of individual CDs to ClO-
ce:section.ce:para: The responsiveness of the two kinds of CDs to ClO- was analyzed before mixing them. ClO- (0–50 μM) was added to R-CDs and G-CDs solutions respectively, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded (Fig S10). As the addition of ClO-, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs almost remains steady, while the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs decreased consecutively, indicating that it is feasible to mix two kinds of CDs for achieve ClO- ratiometric detection.

The result of fluorescence of M-CDs with increasing concentrations of ClO- is shown in Fig. 2. With the addition of ClO-, the fluorescence at 630 nm from R-CDs decreased linearly, while the fluorescence at 525 nm from G-CDs remained stable. The F630/F525 of M-CDs can be effectively droped by ClO- with a linear range (0.1–50 μM) and the detection limit (3Sd/k) of 67 nM can be derived.

For the purpose of investigating the responsiveness of M-CDs to ClO-, the effects of different interferers on fluorescence intensity were measured under the same condition. As can be seen from Fig. 3 A, the F630/F525 ratio quenched by 50 μM ClO- dramatically, while 500 μM of interfering ions were respectively added into the M-CDs solution, and the F630/F525 ratio did not change significantly. Moreover, the coinstantaneous addition of 500 μM interfering ions into the M-CDs solution quenched the F630/F525 ratio by only about 11%. After further addition of 50 μM ClO-, the F630/F525 of M-CDs was vastly reduced (Fig. 3B). The above results indicated that the M-CDs had excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability. In addition, compared with the fluorescent probes shown in Table S1, M-CDs exhibited some better features in ClO- detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed detection mechanism of ClO- based on M-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The dissociation NR was absence in the M-CDs solution and confirmed by Mass spectrometry analysis (Fig S11). The results indicated that the excess NR molecules were removed during the purification of nanoparticles and confirmed that the fluorescence behavior observed in this work came from the particles themselves, rather than any NR remaining in the solution [36]. In the cause of authenticate the mechanism of quenching of fluorescence of R-CDs in the presence of ClO-, Stern-Volmer plot of quenching of R-CDs fluorescence at various concentrations of ClO- was obtained [37–39]. Fig S12A shows that at lower concentrations (0.1 μM-20 μM) the results are in conformity to the Stern-Volmer plot but showed an upward deviate from the initial linearity at higher concentrations which manifested that the quenching process contemporaneously regulated by static quenching and dynamic quenching. The linear part of the Stern-Volmer plot was analyzed using Stern-Volmer equations:
(1)
F
0
/ F − 1 =
K
sv
Q
=
K
q
τ
0
[ Q ]
where F and F
0 are the fluorescence intensities with and without the ClO-, respectively. K
SV is the quenching constant and the slope of the linear regression equation between F
0
/F and ClO- concentration, [Q] is the concentration of ClO-, K
q is the quenching rate constant, and τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs. K SV was calculated to be 1.48 × 104 M−1 with τ0 of 2.04 ns, and K
q was consequently calculated to be 7.25 × 1012 M−1 S−1 for the fluorescence quenching process, that was higher than the minimum possible value of 1 × 1010 M−1 S−1 for static quenching process. Thus, static quenching plays a dominant role in the presence of lower concentrations (0.1 μM-20 μM) of ClO- and the NR fragment on the surface of R-CDs may be oxidized to nitroso compound to form nonfluorescent complexes accompanied by the static quenching of red fluorescence, which in accordance with the shift of absorption peaks in UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig S10C) and the disappearance of hydrogen amino (5.2 ppm) in 1H NMR spectra (Fig S14). The involvement of the dynamic quenching mechanism at higher ClO- concentrations was also demonstrated by temperature-dependent fluorescence behavior (Fig S12B). The K
SV has a positive correlation with temperature due to the enhanced non-radiative collisions deactivation process between ClO- and R-CDs. In addition, the calculated fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs was 2.04 ns, and after adding ClO-, the fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs decreased to 1.60 ns, indicating that R-CDs have a dynamic quenching mechanism. Taken together, we speculate that the NR residues with red fluorescence on the surface of R-CDs were oxidized to non-fluorescent nitroso compounds by ClO- accompanied by the quenching of red fluorescence and a combination of static and dynamic quenching being involved in the process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ClO- in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: ClO- detection in real samples is an important index to evaluate the detection ability of probes. The concentration of ClO- in blank tap water samples was calculated to be 9.26 μM according to the standard curve, which was consistent with the GB5749–2006 sanitary Standard for Drinking Water. As we can see from Table 1, the recovery rates of ClO- in tap water and lake water were 97.2%− 103.4%, indicating that M-CDs have the ability to evaluate ClO- concentration in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ClO- imaging in living cells and zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: Toxicity of M-CDs to cells was assessed by an MTT assay. As shown in Fig S15, as the concentration of M-CDs increase in sequence 0–4, the cell viability was still over 95%, indicating that M-CDs have low cytotoxicity and it has a good application prospect in the field of in vitro imaging. Besides, cellular uptake of G-CDs is more dependent on incubation time, which may be attributed to the larger size of G-CDs (Fig S16). As shown in Fig. 4, with the ClO- concentration increase, the fluorescence intensity of A549 cells in the green channel remained stable, while a noticeable fluorescence decrease was observed in the red channel, which signified that the sensing platform could be used to study the imaging of exogenous ClO- in complex intracellular environment. Then, the imaging capability of M-CDs for endogenous ClO- was investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. 5, as the concentration of hypochlorite stimulant LPS and PMA of incubated cells increased from 0 μg/mL to 2.0 μg/mL, a significant reduction of red fluorescence was observed. However, after the cells were pretreated with 200 μM ABAH to inhibit their myelperoxidase activity, even when the cells were incubated with 2.0 μg/mL LPS and PMA, no significant inhibition of red fluorescence was induced. Therefore, the results indicate that M-CDs can be used to investigate the imaging of endogenous ClO- in cells. As shown in Fig. 6, as the concentration of ClO- increases, the red fluorescence intensity of zebrafish images decreases significantly over time, which suggested that M-CDs could be applied to sense ClO- in vivo.

In conclusion, a carbon-dots-based ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform named M-CDs with NR residue for monitoring ClO- changes in water sample, living cells and zebrafish was dexterously developed through the functional preservation strategy. M-CDs exhibits powerful ability for the monitoring of ClO- in redox homeostasis of the internal environment. The excellent properties of M-CDs including convenient synthesis, photostability and low cytotoxicity make them highly promising analysis tools in the research of oxidative stress and related diseases and brilliant candidates for applications in biological systems.

Yunhao Zhou: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Tian Wu: Formal analysis. Lizheng Duan: Data curation. Guizhen Hu: Formal analysis. Jiahui Shi: Formal analysis. Yamin Nie: Investigation. Yanmei Zhou: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21978069, 21776061).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131946.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reaction-based fluorescent probes for the detection and imaging of reactive oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur species
Acc. Chem. Res.

Sens. Actuators B: Chem.

Yang, Dual-emissive fluorescence and phosphorescence detection of cholesterol and glucose based on carbon dots-cyanuric acid complex quenched by MnO2 nanosheets, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical. 335 (2021) 129715. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.129715.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yunhao Zhou received his bachelor's degree from Henan Institute of Science and Technology. Now, he is a master candidate in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Henan University. His current research interest involves the synthesis and application of carbon dots.

Tian Wu received her M.S. degree in biosensor from Henan Normal University. She is a doctor candidate in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Henan University now. Her current research interest involves the synthesis and application of fluorescent probes.

Lizheng Duan received his bachelor's degree from Henan Kaifeng College of Science Technology and Communication. Now, he is a master candidate in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Henan University. His current research interest involves the synthesis and application of fluorescent probes.

Guizhen Hu received her bachelor's degree from Henan Kaifeng College of Science Technology and Communication. Now, she is a master candidate in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Henan University. His current research interest involves the synthesis and application of fluorescent probes.

Jiahui Shi received her bachelor's degree from Zhongyuan University of Technology. Now, she is a master candidate in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Henan University. His current research interest involves the synthesis and application of carbon dots.

Yamin Nie received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Southwest University. She is working in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Henan University now. Her research interests mainly focus on bioanalysis.

Yanmei Zhou received her Ph.D. degree in environmental engineering from Beijing University of Technology. She is working in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Henan University as a professor now. Her current interests include fluorescent probes, supercapacitor and hydrolysis of cellulose.
